[220:59]in Afghanistan.

[221:24]There's a variety of reasons that you might be against the global war on terror the war in Afghanistan the war in Iraq

[221:30]That is altogether different not completely different, but it is independent from being ideologically

[221:37]against war in itself

[221:40]being ideologically against

[221:43]interventions in general

[221:46]or being an isolationist, which is being against hostile interventions, but also against international

[221:52]cooperation. And I have always said on my show, my foreign policy view is that we should

[222:02]be America first, not isolationist, not even non-interventionist, and I'm not anti-war.

[222:09]I believe that intervention and sometimes up to and including war is a necessary tool.

[222:19]I don't think that that should be the first resort and I don't think that we should overextend

[222:25]ourselves and our commitments to allies or our efforts to undermine adversaries.

[222:37]And I think that sometimes war becomes necessary.

[222:41]I think that it's better, it's preferable to use economic tools and use our international

[222:49]institutions as a forum to mediate disputes and decrease the risk of miscalculations or

[222:57]unnecessary conflicts, things of that nature. But I'm not like a libertarian. I'm not a

[223:02]libertarian that says I hate war. I don't believe we should intervene overseas. There's something

[223:09]in principle wrong with these things. And the reason being is because we are the most

[223:15]powerful nation in the world. We have the biggest Navy. We have the biggest Air Force.

[223:25]and we should use the military to benefit Americans.

[223:30]We should use the military,

[223:32]not to change the way the world is,

[223:35]not to police the world,

[223:37]but we should use the military to secure benefits,

[223:42]tangible benefits for Americans.

[223:44]And what would those things be?

[223:46]Resources.

[223:48]We should secure access to resources

[223:51]that we cannot get.

[223:53]or that make us richer. And we should secure the supply chains that deliver them. The other

[223:59]reason we should use the military is for, of course, defending the homeland. And to me,

[224:06]that comes in two distinct forms, which is our immediate periphery, which is the Western

[224:15]But also the proliferation of nuclear weapons because if there's a nuclear war, that's not good for anybody

[224:20]So that this is kind of generally where I'm at and I say that to preface because

[224:25]This is why it's difficult when you approach the Venezuela conversation because for 10 years

[224:31]I have been opposed and and vocally and adamantly opposed to our interventions in the Middle East

[224:46]opposed to all that. And you start to sound like you're a libertarian, or you start to

[224:51]sound like you're a non-interventionist, when really I'm not. And when it comes to Venezuela,

[224:57]I see in this case, for me, it's a little more complicated because this is a legitimate

[225:10]The entire geo strategic posture of the United States is based on the fact or what's based

[225:17]on the geographic reality that the United States is protected by natural barriers.

[225:29]We don't think the same way as other countries because we don't share a border with other

[225:37]like, let's say Russia or Germany or India or Iran, we're protected by two oceans and

[225:46]there are no other countries in the North and South American continents that threaten

[225:51]us. And so in order for us to remain safe, we don't really need to worry as much about

[225:57]what is happening on the Eurasian African continent. That's not an immediate threat

[226:04]Because we're protected from that by the oceans.

[226:07]What we do have to worry about is our island.

[226:11]We do have to worry about is the Caribbean.

[226:13]We have to worry about Central America and South America.

[226:17]Because that is what is close.

[226:19]That is what has a land bridge to the United States.

[226:22]That is in our immediate periphery.

[226:24]And that's why we have the Monroe Doctrine,

[226:26]which says that for America to be safe,

[226:29]It is our legitimate sphere of influence. It is our legitimate jurisdiction to govern

[226:35]the Americas. And we don't necessarily need to intervene in the balance of power in Europe

[226:41]or in Asia or in the Middle East. And there's not too much happening in Africa. But what

[227:00]the United States, ideologically aligned against the United States ever since the Bolivarian
